title: Onion Bhaji

overview.description: Crisp, deeply flavoured Indian onion fritters made with thinly sliced onions, gram flour and aromatic spices.

ingredients: ½ tsp chilli powder, or to taste
method: Place the sliced onions in a large bowl.
Add the salt and mix thoroughly.
Leave for 15–20 minutes so the salt draws moisture from the onions.
Add the gram flour, cumin, coriander, turmeric, garam masala, chilli powder and baking powder.
Add the fresh coriander and green chilli if using.
Mix thoroughly until the onions are coated.
Add the water a little at a time, mixing between additions.
Stop adding water as soon as the mixture holds together when pressed.
Take small handfuls of the mixture and gently drop them into the hot oil.
Fry in batches, taking care not to overcrowd the pan.
Cook for approximately 4–6 minutes, turning occasionally, until deep golden brown and crisp.
Drain on a wire rack or kitchen paper.

learning.did-you-know: The word bhaji refers broadly to a vegetable preparation, while onion bhaji is one of the best-known examples outside South Asia.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How salt draws moisture from onions and how a minimal amount of water can bind a fritter mixture without making the finished bhaji heavy.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: An inexpensive, freezer-friendly pantry-based snack that can also serve as a starter, side dish or accompaniment to Indian meals.
chapman-tips: Slice the onions thinly so they cook through before the outside becomes too dark.
Salting the onions before adding the gram flour helps create a cohesive mixture with less water.
The mixture should be sticky and clumpy rather than wet and runny.
Avoid making the bhajis too large or the centre may remain soft while the outside browns.
A wire rack helps preserve crispness better than placing the bhajis directly on kitchen paper.
Don't overcrowd the oil or the temperature will drop too much.
The spice level can be increased without changing the structure of the recipe.
A small amount of fennel seed is also excellent in the mixture.
For a lighter texture, separate the onion strands slightly as you form each bhaji.
notes.batch-cooking: Better fresh. Can be frozen after frying, although the texture is best when freshly cooked.
notes.leftovers: Reheat in an oven or air fryer to restore crispness. Avoid reheating in the microwave if possible.
